bibliographic_data.title: Solvent engineering for scalable fabrication of perovskite/silicon tandem solar cells in air
bibliographic_data.abstract: Perovskite/silicon tandem solar cells hold great promise for realizing high power conversion efficiency at low cost. However, achieving scalable fabrication of wide-bandgap perovskite (~1.68 eV) in air remains challenging due to moisture-induced degradation of perovskite films. This study reveals that the extent of moisture interference is significantly influenced by the properties of solvent. We further demonstrate that n-Butanol (nBA), with its low polarity and moderate volatilization rate, not only mitigates the detrimental effects of moisture in air during scalable fabrication but also enhances the uniformity of perovskite films. This approach enables us to achieve an impressive efficiency of 29.4% (certified 28.7%) for double-sided textured perovskite/silicon tandem cells featuring large-size pyramids (2–3 μm) and 26.3% over an aperture area of 16 cm².

core_content.novelty_claim: The study proposes a solvent engineering strategy using n-Butanol to mitigate the detrimental effects of moisture in air during scalable fabrication of perovskite/silicon tandem solar cells, achieving high efficiency and uniformity.
core_content.key_methodology: The research employs a two-step sequential deposition method combining co-evaporation and blade-coating techniques. The key solvent used is n-Butanol (nBA), which has low polarity and moderate volatilization rate, to enhance the quality and uniformity of perovskite films.
outcomes: Achieved an efficiency of 29.4% for double-sided textured perovskite/silicon tandem cells with large-size pyramids (2–3 μm)
Achieved a conversion efficiency of 26.3% over an aperture area of 16 cm²
Enabled scalable fabrication in air without the need for inert atmosphere
core_content.full_text_markdown: Perovskite/silicon tandem solar cells are highly efficient and cost-effective, making them prime candidates for industrialization. However, achieving scalable fabrication of wide-bandgap perovskites (~1.68 eV) in air remains challenging due to moisture-induced degradation. This study reveals that the extent of moisture interference is significantly influenced by the properties of solvents. The research proposes using n-Butanol (nBA), which has low polarity and moderate volatilization rate, to mitigate these effects during scalable fabrication. This approach enhances the uniformity of perovskite films and enables achieving high efficiencies: 29.4% for double-sided textured cells with large pyramids (2–3 μm) and 26.3% over an aperture area of 16 cm², marking a significant stride toward commercial viability.
